items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils often nutrient-poor despite abundant vegetation?
items.correct: Rapid decomposition and uptake mean nutrients cycle quickly through living biomass, not soil.
items.misconception: Rainforests have rich soils because they are productive.
items.random_wrong: Rainforests are deserts most of the year.
items.why: Students assume productive = rich soil. Actually most nutrients in rainforests are in living biomass; rapid uptake leaves soils thin. That's why clear-cutting destroys long-term productivity quickly.

items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.

items.q: The Paris Agreement was adopted in:
items.why: Correct answer: 2015. “2005” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: IPCC AR6 best estimate of climate sensitivity (degrees C):
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 3. “1.5” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Year the Paris Agreement was adopted at COP21?
items.why: The real figure is 2015 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
